sub_topics.name: Overview of British Government Structure
sub_topics.description: This module provides a summarized overview of the core components and institutions of the British government, including the monarch, parliament, and the prime minister's role.

sub_1_f1468c.title: Introduction to British Government Structure
sub_1_f1468c.objective: Understand the basic components of the British government structure.
sub_1_f1468c.summary: Understand the basic components of the British government structure.
sub_1_f1468c.coverage_contract: Provide a high-level overview of the main components of the British government structure.
sub_1_f1468c.goal: Set the stage for understanding the structure of the British government.
sub_1_f1468c.reasoning: This slide is needed to provide learners with a foundational understanding of the British government structure, allowing for deeper exploration in subsequent slides.
key_facts: The UK is a constitutional monarchy with a parliamentary democracy.
The monarch's role is mostly ceremonial, with real power exercised by elected officials.
The UK Parliament is the supreme legal authority, consisting of two houses: the House of Commons and the House of Lords.
sub_1_f1468c.assessment_prompt: What are the two main components of the UK Parliament?
sub_1_f1468c.research_raw_text:
📚 VERIFIED FACTS (Source: WIKIPEDIA):
Confidence: HIGH

The Government of British Columbia (French: Gouvernement de la Colombie-Britannique) is the body responsible for the administration of the Canadian province of British Columbia. The term Government of British Columbia can refer to either the collective set of all three institutions, or more specifically to the executive—ministers of the Crown (the Executive Council) of the day, and the non-political staff within each provincial department or agency, i.e. the civil services, whom the ministers direct—which corporately brands itself as the Government of British Columbia, or more formally, His Majesty's Government (French: Gouvernement de Sa Majesté).
The current construct was established when the province joined Confederation in 1871. British Columbia is a secondary jurisdiction of Canada, a constitutional monarchy with a parliamentary democracy in the Westminster tradition; a premier—David Eby of the New Democratic Party since 2022—is the head of government and is invited by the Crown to form a government after securing the confidence of the Legislative Assembly, typically determined through the election of enough members of the Legislative Assembly (MLAs) of a single political party in an election to provide a majority of seats, forming a governing party or coalition. The sovereign is King Charles III, Canada's head of state, who is represented provincially in British Columbia by the lieutenant governor, Wendy Lisogar-Cocchia since 2025.


Role of the Crown

King Charles III, as sovereign, is also the King in Right of British Columbia. As a Commonwealth realm, the Canadian monarch is shared with 14 other independent countries within the Commonwealth of Nations. Within Canada, the monarch exercises power individually on behalf of the federal government and the ten provinces.
The powers of the Crown are vested in the monarch and are exercised by the lieutenant governor. The advice of the premier and Executive Council is typically binding; the Constitution Act, 1867 requires executive power to be exercised only "by and with the advice of the Executive Council".


Lieutenant governor

The lieutenant governor is appointed by the governor general, on the advice of the prime minister of Canada. Thus, it is typically the lieutenant governor whom the premier and ministers advise, exercising much of the royal prerogative and granting royal assent.
The executive power is vested in the Crown and exercised in-Council, meaning on the advice of the Executive Council; conventionally, this is the Cabinet, which is chaired by the premier and comprises ministers of the Crown.


Premier and Executive Council

The term Government of British Columbia, or more formally His Majesty's Government, refers to the activities of the King-in-Council. The day-to-day operation and activities of the Government of British Columbia are performed by the provincial departments and agencies, staffed by the non-partisan public service and directed by the elected government.


Premier

The premier of British Columbia is the primary minister of the Crown. The premier acts as the head of government for the province, chairs and selects the membership of the Cabinet, and advises the Crown on the exercise of executive power and much of the royal prerogative. As premiers hold office by virtue of their ability to command the confidence of the elected Legislative Assembly, they typically sit as a MLA and lead the largest party or a coalition in the Assembly. Once sworn in, the premier holds office until either they resign or they are removed by the lieutenant governor after either a motion of no confidence or defeat in a general election.
David Eby has been premier since November 18, 2022, after winning the NDP leadership election. He succeeded John Horgan, who led the New Democratic Party to a majority government in the 2020 British Columbia general election.


See also
Politics of British Columbia
Politics of Canada and Government of Canada


References


Works cited
Brooks, Stephen (2007). Canadian Democracy: An Introduction (5 ed.). Don Mills: Oxford University Press. ISBN 978-0-19-543103-2.


External links
Official website 
Organizational structure of the BC government; on gov.bc.ca
Government 101: How Government Works; on gov.bc.ca
Source URL: https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Government_of_British_Columbia

sub_1_f1468c.title: The Role of the Monarchy
sub_1_f1468c.objective: Recognize the ceremonial and constitutional roles of the British monarch.
sub_1_f1468c.summary: Recognize the ceremonial and constitutional roles of the British monarch.
sub_1_f1468c.coverage_contract: Explain the constitutional and ceremonial roles of the British monarch.
sub_1_f1468c.goal: Clarify the specific roles and limitations of the monarchy within the UK government.
sub_1_f1468c.reasoning: This slide provides clarity on the monarch's role, which is often misunderstood, emphasizing the distinction between ceremonial duties and actual political power.
key_facts: The British monarch performs ceremonial duties like the State Opening of Parliament.
The monarch's constitutional role includes the formal appointment of the Prime Minister.
While the monarch has the right to be consulted, advise, and warn, real political power is limited.
sub_1_f1468c.assessment_prompt: What are two ceremonial duties performed by the British monarch?

sub_1_f1468c.title: The UK Parliament: Structure and Function
sub_1_f1468c.objective: Identify the structure and roles of the UK Parliament's two houses.
sub_1_f1468c.summary: Identify the structure and roles of the UK Parliament's two houses.
sub_1_f1468c.coverage_contract: Detail the composition and functions of the House of Commons and the House of Lords.
sub_1_f1468c.goal: Provide a detailed understanding of the roles and differences between the two houses of Parliament.
sub_1_f1468c.reasoning: This slide is necessary to distinguish the unique functions and powers of each house, highlighting their roles in the legislative process.
key_facts: The House of Commons is composed of Members of Parliament (MPs) elected by the public.
The House of Lords includes appointed members, bishops, and hereditary peers.
Both houses must agree on legislation for it to become law, with the Commons holding more power.
sub_1_f1468c.assessment_prompt: How does the legislative process differ between the House of Commons and the House of Lords?

The sovereign is King Charles III, Canada's head of state, who is represented provincially in British Columbia by the lieutenant governor, Wendy Lisogar-Cocchia since 2025.


Role of the Crown

King Charles III, as sovereign, is also the King in Right of British Columbia. As a Commonwealth realm, the Canadian monarch is shared with 14 other independent countries within the Commonwealth of Nations. Within Canada, the monarch exercises power individually on behalf of the federal government and the ten provinces.
The powers of the Crown are vested in the monarch and are exercised by the lieutenant governor. The advice of the premier and Executive Council is typically binding; the Constitution Act, 1867 requires executive power to be exercised only "by and with the advice of the Executive Council".


Lieutenant governor

The lieutenant governor is appointed by the governor general, on the advice of the prime minister of Canada. Thus, it is typically the lieutenant governor whom the premier and ministers advise, exercising much of the royal prerogative and granting royal assent.
The executive power is vested in the Crown and exercised in-Council, meaning on the advice of the Executive Council; conventionally, this is the Cabinet, which is chaired by the premier and comprises ministers of the Crown.


Premier and Executive Council

The term Government of British Columbia, or more formally His Majesty's Government, refers to the activities of the King-in-Council. The day-to-day operation and activities of the Government of British Columbia are performed by the provincial departments and agencies, staffed by the non-partisan public service and directed by the elected government.


Premier

The premier of British Columbia is the primary minister of the Crown. The premier acts as the head of government for the province, chairs and selects the membership of the Cabinet, and advises the Crown on the exercise of executive power and much of the royal prerogative. As premiers hold office by virtue of their ability to command the confidence of the elected Legislative Assembly, they typically sit as a MLA and lead the largest party or a coalition in the Assembly. Once sworn in, the premier holds office until either they resign or they are removed by the lieutenant governor after either a motion of no confidence or defeat in a general election.
David Eby has been premier since November 18, 2022, after winning the NDP leadership election. He succeeded John Horgan, who led the New Democratic Party to a majority government in the 2020 British Columbia general election.

Politics of British Columbia
Politics of Canada and Government of Canada

Brooks, Stephen (2007). Canadian Democracy: An Introduction (5 ed.). Don Mills: Oxford University Press. ISBN 978-0-19-543103-2.

sub_1_f1468c.narration_text:
To understand the intricacies of the British government, it's essential to recognize that the United Kingdom operates as a constitutional monarchy, which means it combines elements of monarchy with a parliamentary system, providing a unique balance of power and representation. This blend is crucial for the functioning of the government, as it ensures that while there is a monarch, the actual governing is carried out by elected officials.

The real power in the British government is exercised by these elected officials within the parliamentary system. This system allows for a democratic process where members of parliament are elected by the people, and they, in turn, make decisions on behalf of their constituents. The parliamentary democracy is the backbone of the British political system, enabling the representation of various interests and the implementation of policies that reflect the will of the people.

The role of the monarch in this system is largely ceremonial, with very limited executive power. While the monarch serves as the head of state, their duties are mostly symbolic and do not involve direct involvement in the day-to-day governance of the country. This distinction is important, as it separates the symbolic, unifying figure of the monarch from the practical, political decisions made by the elected government, ensuring a clear division of responsibilities and preventing potential conflicts of interest.
